1.pericope_label: The Promise of the Holy Spirit
1.synthesis: Acts opens by tying itself to Luke's Gospel: "In the first book… I have dealt with all that Jesus began to do and teach, until the day when he was taken up" (vv.1–2). The risen Jesus had presented himself alive "by many proofs," appearing over forty days and speaking of the kingdom of God (v.3). Now he charges the apostles not to leave Jerusalem but to "wait for the promise of the Father," for "you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit not many days from now" (vv.4–5). The book's whole engine is set in these verses: the ascended Christ will pour out the Spirit so that his work may continue through his witnesses.
1.historical_context: The address to "most excellent Theophilus" (cf. Luke 1:3) and the reference to a "first book" (v.1) place Acts in the genre of Hellenistic historiography and bind Luke-Acts into the longest single work in the New Testament. The forty days of resurrection appearances (v.3) evoke the formative forty-day periods of Israel's story — Moses on Sinai (Exodus 24:18), Elijah to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8) — a season of formation and transition. The forty days before Pentecost also fit the count from Passover to Shavuot (Leviticus 23:15–16), the feast that celebrated the giving of the Law at Sinai; the Spirit's arrival on that day signals the new-covenant law written on hearts (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26–27).
1.literary_structure: The phrase "all that Jesus began to do and teach" (v.1) is programmatic: the Gospel recorded the beginning, and Acts records what Jesus continues to do by the Spirit through the church, so the risen Lord remains the hidden protagonist of every chapter. The prologue recapitulates the Gospel's ending — the resurrection appearances and the command to wait (cf. Luke 24:49) — overlapping the two volumes at the ascension. The contrast "John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit" (v.5) reaches back to the Baptist's own promise (Luke 3:16) and points forward to its fulfillment at Pentecost.
1.theological_application: The Christian mission begins not with activity but with waiting on God's gift: the apostles are told to stay in Jerusalem until they are clothed with power (v.4), teaching that fruitful service flows from the Spirit's enabling, not human zeal. The "many proofs" of the resurrection (v.3) ground faith in evidence, not wishful thinking — the strongest Greek word for conclusive demonstration. And the Spirit is named "the promise of the Father" (v.4): the believer's empowerment is a gift to be received in dependence, the keeping of a divine pledge.
1.christology: The risen Jesus is the giver of the Holy Spirit (vv.4–5) — a divine prerogative, for the Spirit is the Father's own — fulfilling his promise of "another Helper" (John 14:16). That Acts records what Jesus "began" and now continues (v.1) confesses him as the living, reigning Lord who acts in history through his church. The forty days of teaching about the kingdom (v.3) bind the crucified and risen One to the message he proclaimed before his death: the same Jesus, now alive forevermore, directs the mission from heaven.

6.synthesis: The disciples ask, "Lord, will you at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?" (v.6). Jesus redirects them from times and seasons to mission: "You will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth" (v.8). Then, as they watch, he is lifted up and a cloud takes him from their sight (v.9); two men in white promise, "This Jesus… will come in the same way as you saw him go into heaven" (v.11). The ascension enthrones the risen Lord and sets the agenda — and the geography — of the entire book.
6.historical_context: The disciples' question (v.6) reflects standard Second Temple hope: the Davidic kingdom restored, Rome expelled, Israel vindicated, drawing on texts like the rebuilt "booth of David" (Amos 9:11). The commission's geography — Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the ends of the earth (v.8) — is also a crossing of social boundaries, over the bitter Jewish-Samaritan rift and out to the Gentiles, echoing the Servant who is "a light… to the end of the earth" (Isaiah 49:6). The receiving cloud (v.9) is the Old Testament vehicle of God's presence — the Shekinah of Sinai and the Transfiguration — and recalls the Son of Man who comes "with the clouds" (Daniel 7:13). The Mount of Olives (v.12) was linked in prophecy with the Lord's end-time coming (Zechariah 14:4).
6.literary_structure: Verse 8 is the table of contents for all of Acts: the gospel will advance from Jerusalem (chs. 1–7) through Judea and Samaria (chs. 8–12) to the ends of the earth, reaching Rome (chs. 13–28). The disciples' backward-looking question about restoring Israel (v.6) is answered by a forward-looking commission, reframing a national hope as a worldwide mission. The ascension scene (vv.9–11) forms the hinge between the two volumes — narrated briefly at the close of the Gospel (Luke 24:51) and again here — and the angels' promise of a like return frames the whole age of witness between Christ's going and his coming again.
6.theological_application: Jesus gently corrects a perennial temptation — the obsession with calculating end-time "times or seasons" (v.7) — and turns the church's gaze instead to its task: to be witnesses by the Spirit's power (v.8). The angels' rebuke to those "gazing into heaven" (v.11) likewise redirects nostalgia and speculation into mission and hope. The promise that the same Jesus will return "in the same way" anchors the church's labor between the certainty of his reign and the certainty of his coming, so that witness is carried out in confident expectation.
6.christology: The ascension is not a sad departure but an enthronement: the cloud that receives Jesus (v.9) signals his exaltation to the divine throne, the Son of Man brought before the Ancient of Days to receive dominion (Daniel 7:13–14). From there he pours out the Spirit and directs his witnesses, the reigning Lord of the mission. The angelic word that "this Jesus" — the same crucified, risen, ascended person — "will come in the same way" (v.11) guarantees a personal, bodily return, so the church's hope is fixed not on a vague providence but on the Christ who left and will come again.

12.synthesis: The apostles return to the upper room and devote themselves to prayer with the women, Mary the mother of Jesus, and his brothers (vv.12–14). In a gathering of about 120, Peter rises to address the matter of Judas, "who became a guide to those who arrested Jesus" and met a grim end in the Field of Blood (vv.15–19). He reads Judas's defection and the vacancy he left as foretold in the Psalms: "Let another take his office" (v.20). The waiting community prepares, in prayer and Scripture, to restore the broken circle of the Twelve.
12.historical_context: The Mount of Olives lay "a Sabbath day's journey" (about 880 meters) from the city (v.12), returning the disciples to the symbolic center of Jewish life. The presence of Jesus' brothers (v.14) is remarkable, for during his ministry his family did not believe (John 7:5); the resurrection appearances, including one to James (1 Corinthians 15:7), transformed them. The number 120 (v.15) carries weight — twelve times ten, and in Jewish tradition the minimum population for a town to have its own Sanhedrin — marking a community constituted as a complete Israel. Luke's account of Judas's death (v.18) supplements Matthew's (Matthew 27:5), and "Akeldama," the Field of Blood, was a known Jerusalem site behind both traditions.
12.literary_structure: This scene establishes the pattern that will recur throughout Acts: the church gathered in united, persistent prayer (v.14) before each decisive advance. Peter's emergence as spokesman (v.15) continues his designated role (cf. Luke 22:32) and anticipates his Pentecost sermon. The handling of Judas is built on the early Christian reading of the Psalms as Davidic prophecy: Psalms 69:25 and Psalms 109:8 are applied to the betrayer (v.20), with the "office" (episkopē) language of the latter providing the warrant for a successor.
12.theological_application: The infant church waits in prayer (v.14), modeling the dependence that should precede every act of obedience — they do not improvise but seek God together. The transformed presence of Jesus' once-unbelieving family (v.14) is a quiet testimony to the resurrection's power to change the hardest skeptics. And Peter's confidence that "the Scripture had to be fulfilled" (v.16) shows a community learning to read even tragedy and betrayal within the sovereign purposes of God revealed in his word.
12.christology: The reading of the Psalms here assumes that David spoke prophetically of the Christ and his enemies, so that Judas becomes the type of the betrayer of the Anointed One (v.20). The concern to restore the Twelve testifies to Jesus' purpose in constituting a renewed Israel around himself: the apostolic college is the foundation of the messianic people, and its completeness matters because it represents the twelve tribes gathered under their Lord. Even Judas's fall, foreseen in Scripture, is woven into the unfolding of the Messiah's appointed path.

21.synthesis: Peter sets the qualification for the vacant apostleship: it must be one of the men who accompanied them "all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us, beginning from the baptism of John until the day when he was taken up" — "one of these men must become with us a witness to his resurrection" (vv.21–22). Two are put forward, Joseph called Barsabbas and Matthias; the community prays to the Lord "who knows the hearts of all" to show his choice (vv.23–24), casts lots, and the lot falls to Matthias, who is numbered with the eleven (v.26). The Twelve are made whole on the eve of Pentecost.
21.historical_context: The criterion of eyewitness presence through the whole arc "from the baptism of John" to the ascension (vv.21–22) defines apostleship by firsthand testimony to Jesus — which is why Paul, who was not such a witness, would later argue strenuously for his Damascus-road encounter as a true resurrection appearance (1 Corinthians 9:1). The title invoked in prayer, "Lord, who knows the hearts of all" (kardiognōstēs, v.24), is a divine attribute (cf. Acts 15:8). Casting lots (v.26) was a recognized Jewish means of discerning God's will — "the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD" (Proverbs 16:33; cf. Leviticus 16:8) — and this is the last lot cast in the New Testament.
21.literary_structure: The selection completes the restoration of the Twelve begun in the previous section, deliberately staged before Pentecost so that the renewed people of God is whole when the Spirit comes. The prayer (vv.24–25) frames the lot not as chance but as the means by which God reveals a choice he has already made — "show which one of these two you have chosen." Notably, this is the final instance of lot-casting in Acts: after the Spirit is poured out, the church discerns leadership through the Spirit's guidance and communal recognition rather than the lot, marking a turning point in the book's account of divine guidance.
21.theological_application: The church seeks God's will through Scripture, prayer, and the appointed means at hand, trusting that "the Lord who knows all hearts" (v.24) directs the outcome. The episode dignifies faithful, long-obedient discipleship: the qualification is not brilliance but having been present with Jesus through the whole journey (v.21). That Matthias, once chosen, never appears again by name is itself instructive — faithful service often goes unrecorded, its significance known fully only to the Lord who reads the heart.
21.christology: The defining task of the new apostle is to be "a witness to his resurrection" (v.22): the risen Christ stands at the center of the apostolic office and the church's entire testimony. Strikingly, the community prays to Jesus as the "Lord" who knows all hearts and chooses his servants (v.24) — a divine title and prerogative applied to him, an early and unselfconscious confession of his deity. The completion of the Twelve under his lordship affirms that the Messiah is even now gathering and ordering his renewed people in preparation for the Spirit's coming.
